topic: Unit 3: Extended Writing - Film and Literature
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'I am eating bread' (some bread) in French?
items.random_wrong: Je mange le pain (which means 'the bread').
items.why: Students drop the partitive. French requires DU/DE LA/DES for 'some' even when English omits it. Skipping it makes the sentence ungrammatical in French.

items.q: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'nous'?
items.why: Students apply the same ending across persons. ER verbs use different endings: je parle, tu parles, il parle, NOUS parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. Each subject takes its own ending.
A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.
The answer examiners want is: Meursault. “Rieux” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which form is the correct imparfait of 'être' for 'il'?
items.why: It's était. “est” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Camus win the Nobel Prize for Literature?
items.why: Exact answer: 1957. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
